paper.title: Constrained Two-step Look-Ahead Bayesian Optimization
paper.abstract: Recent advances in computationally efficient non-myopic Bayesian optimization offer improved query efficiency over traditional myopic methods like expected improvement, with only a modest increase in computational cost. These advances have been largely limited to unconstrained BO methods with only a few exceptions which require heavy computation. For instance, one existing multi-step lookahead constrained BO method (Lam & Willcox, 2017) relies on computationally expensive unreliable brute force derivative-free optimization of a Monte Carlo rollout acquisition function. Methods that use the reparameterization trick for more efficient derivative-based optimization of non-myopic acquisition functions in the unconstrained setting, like sample average approximation and infinitesimal perturbation analysis, do not extend: constraints introduce discontinuities in the sampled acquisition function surface. Moreover, we argue here that being non-myopic is even more important in constrained problems because fear of violating constraints pushes myopic methods away from sampling the boundary between feasible and infeasible regions, slowing the discovery of optimal solutions with tight constraints. In this paper, we propose a computationally efficient two-step lookahead constrained Bayesian optimization acquisition function (2-OPT-C) supporting both sequential and batch settings. To enable fast acquisition function optimization, we develop a novel likelihood ratio-based unbiased estimator of the gradient of the two-step optimal acquisition function that does not use the reparameterization trick. In numerical experiments, 2-OPT-C typically improves query efficiency by 2x or more over previous methods, and in some cases by 10x or more.

review_fields.summary: This work suggests a two-step lookahead Bayesian optimization strategy in the presence of inequality constraints, which is non-myopic and computationally efficient. The previous work [1] applies unreliable derivative-free optimization of acquisition function in such a constrained optimization problem, but the method proposed in this paper lets us to leverage a gradient-based optimization framework in a two-step lookahead Bayesian optimization strategy. Moreover, the proposed method supports either a sequential setting or a batch setting. Finally, the authors show the differentiability of the proposed acquisition function and experimental results.
review_fields.main_review:
This paper solves an interesting topic, which can be applied in many practical scenarios in the presence of constraints. Considering a constrained optimization problem with a non-myopic approach is pretty important and the method using a gradient-based optimization framework has the advantage of computational efficiency. My only concern is about numerical experiments. I think real-world experiments should be added in order to demonstrate the usefulness of the proposed method.

Pros

+ Interesting topic.
+ Well-written and well-organized paper.
+ Clear description of contributions

Cons

- No real-world experiments.

Detailed Comments

1. I would like to see the benefit of the proposed method in terms of computational cost, compared to other existing methods.
2. How many times did you repeat the experiments? I cannot find it in the paper.
3. I would like to ask if non-myopic approaches are always better than myopic approaches. I do not think that it needs to describe in the paper, but I was just curious about it.

Minor issues

* In Line 123, q should be $q$.
* For Equation (2), a period should be added.
* For the equations in Line 155 and Line 161, commas should be added.
* In Line 191, q should be $q$.

[1] R. Lam and K. Willcox. Lookahead Bayesian optimization with inequality constraints. In Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, volume 30, pages 1890–1900, 2017.
review_fields.limitations_and_societal_impact: I do not have any comments on limitations and societal impact. Please see "Main Review" for the detailed reviews.
review_fields.ethical_concerns: I do not have any ethical concerns.
